svg.text: Four critical embedding failure modes

g.onclick: sendPrompt('Why does high cosine similarity not guarantee a section answers the query?')

svg.text: Query: "Who was driving the car that hit Myrtle?"
Rank 1 (sim 0.87): "The car struck Myrtle with full force..."
→ Describes the crash. Does NOT name the driver.
Rank 3 (sim 0.79): "Daisy, at the wheel, had…" ← Answer
Bi-encoders encode independently — cannot verify
whether the specific answer exists in the passage

g.onclick: sendPrompt('How does chunk size affect embedding retrieval quality?')

svg.text: Too large (1000+ tokens)
Multi-topic → diluted centroid → weak signal
Optimal: 256–512 tokens (factoid queries)
Section-aligned, self-contained, heading-anchored
Too small (<50 tokens)
Matches surface features, lacks answer context

g.onclick: sendPrompt('How does keyword stuffing cause false positives in embedding retrieval?')

svg.text: Section stuffed with: "API integration, API error,
API rate limit, API timeout, API auth, API call…"
Scores high vs "API error codes" — contains no answers
Dense keyword co-occurrence → cluster proximity
in embedding space. Wastes LLM context window.

g.onclick: sendPrompt('Why cant I use a fixed cosine similarity threshold for relevance?')

svg.text: Same cosine score 0.82 can mean:
Strong relevance in corpus A
Weak relevance in corpus B (different distribution)
Contrastive training compresses scores
into 0.6–1.0. Only rank order is valid.
Fix for all four: use a two-stage pipeline — embedding retrieval narrows candidates, LLM verifies answers
Embeddings measure topical proximity. LLMs measure answer quality. Both are required.
